sections.title: Financial Reporting Standards - Overview
sections.content:
WHAT THIS MEANS: Financial reporting standards are the 'rules of the game' for how companies prepare their financial statements. These standards ensure that financial statements are consistent, comparable, and reliable.

WHY THIS MATTERS FOR THE EXAM: FSA represents 13-17% of the CFA Level I exam (approximately 23-31 questions out of 180). This is the SECOND HIGHEST weighted topic. Understanding financial statements is fundamental to analyzing investments.

TWO MAJOR FRAMEWORKS:

FeatureIFRSUS GAAP
Full NameInternational Financial Reporting StandardsU.S. Generally Accepted Accounting Principles
Issued ByIASB (International Accounting Standards Board)FASB (Financial Accounting Standards Board)
ApproachPrinciples-based (general guidelines, more judgment)Rules-based (specific rules, less judgment)
Used In140+ countries including EU, Australia, CanadaPrimarily United States
Inventory (LIFO)LIFO is PROHIBITEDLIFO is ALLOWED

THE FOUR MAIN FINANCIAL STATEMENTS:

  1. Balance Sheet (Statement of Financial Position): Shows what the company OWNS (assets) and OWES (liabilities) at a specific point in time. Also shows shareholders' equity. FORMULA: Assets = Liabilities + Equity
  2. Income Statement (Statement of Profit or Loss): Shows the company's REVENUES and EXPENSES over a period of time (e.g., one year). Shows whether the company made a profit or loss.
  3. Cash Flow Statement: Shows how cash moved in and out of the company during a period. Divided into: Operating activities, Investing activities, Financing activities.
  4. Statement of Changes in Equity: Shows changes in shareholders' equity over a period.

ACCRUAL ACCOUNTING vs. CASH ACCOUNTING:

  • Accrual Accounting (REQUIRED for financial statements): Record revenue when EARNED (not when cash is received). Record expenses when INCURRED (not when cash is paid). EXAMPLE: You deliver goods in December 2024 but don't get paid until January 2025. Under accrual accounting, you record the revenue in DECEMBER 2024 (when earned).
  • Cash Accounting: Record revenue when CASH IS RECEIVED. Record expenses when CASH IS PAID. EXAMPLE: Using the same scenario, you'd record revenue in JANUARY 2025 (when cash received). This is NOT allowed for financial statements but is used for some tax purposes.

EXAM MEMORY TIP: Remember 'BARC' - Balance sheet, Activity (income), Revenue/expense matching, Cash flow statement. These are the four main statements.
keyPoints: FSA = 13-17% of exam (23-31 questions) - SECOND HIGHEST weighted topic
IFRS is principles-based, used in 140+ countries
US GAAP is rules-based, used primarily in United States
IFRS prohibits LIFO inventory method; US GAAP allows it
Four main statements: Balance Sheet, Income Statement, Cash Flow, Changes in Equity
Balance Sheet formula: Assets = Liabilities + Equity
Accrual accounting records revenue when EARNED, not when cash received
Accrual accounting is REQUIRED for financial statements
Memory aid: 'BARC' - Balance sheet, Activity, Revenue/expense, Cash flow

practiceQuestions.question: A company delivers products to a customer in December 2024 but doesn't receive payment until January 2025. Under accrual accounting, when should the revenue be recognized?
options: December 2024, when the products were delivered
Either December 2024 or January 2025, at management's discretion
practiceQuestions.explanation: Under accrual accounting, revenue is recognized when EARNED (when products are delivered), not when cash is received. This is the revenue recognition principle. Even though cash wasn't received until January, the company earned the revenue in December by delivering the products. EXAM TIP: This is a FUNDAMENTAL concept - accrual accounting focuses on when economic activity occurs, not when cash changes hands.

practiceQuestions.question: Which of the following is a KEY DIFFERENCE between IFRS and US GAAP?
options: IFRS is rules-based while US GAAP is principles-based
IFRS prohibits LIFO inventory method while US GAAP allows it
IFRS is used only in Europe while US GAAP is used worldwide
practiceQuestions.explanation: IFRS prohibits the LIFO (Last-In, First-Out) inventory method, while US GAAP allows it. This is one of the most important differences between the two frameworks. Option A is backwards - IFRS is principles-based, US GAAP is rules-based. Option C is wrong - IFRS is used in 140+ countries worldwide, not just Europe. EXAM TIP: LIFO prohibition is a VERY common exam question on IFRS vs. US GAAP differences.

practiceQuestions.question: The balance sheet equation states that:
practiceQuestions.explanation: The fundamental balance sheet equation is: Assets = Liabilities + Equity. This equation must ALWAYS balance, which is why it's called a 'balance sheet.' Everything the company owns (assets) is financed either by creditors (liabilities) or by owners (equity). EXAM TIP: This is THE most fundamental accounting equation. You must know this cold for the exam.

sections.title: Income Statement Analysis
sections.content:
WHAT THIS MEANS: The income statement shows whether a company made money (profit) or lost money (loss) over a period of time. It's like a 'video' of the company's performance, not a 'snapshot' like the balance sheet.

THE INCOME STATEMENT STRUCTURE (Top to Bottom):

  1. Revenue (Sales): Money coming in from selling goods or services. This is the 'top line.'
  2. Cost of Goods Sold (COGS): Direct costs of producing the goods or services sold. EXAMPLE: For a car manufacturer, COGS includes steel, tires, labor to assemble cars.
  3. Gross Profit: Revenue minus COGS. This shows how much profit the company makes on its core business BEFORE overhead costs. FORMULA: Gross Profit = Revenue - COGS
  4. Operating Expenses: Indirect costs not directly tied to production. EXAMPLES: Sales salaries, rent, utilities, marketing, R&D, administrative salaries.
  5. Operating Income (EBIT): Gross profit minus operating expenses. This shows profit from CORE OPERATIONS before interest and taxes. FORMULA: Operating Income = Gross Profit - Operating Expenses
  6. Interest Expense: Cost of borrowing money (interest on debt).
  7. Pre-Tax Income (EBT): Operating income minus interest expense. FORMULA: EBT = Operating Income - Interest
  8. Income Tax Expense: Taxes owed to the government.
  9. Net Income: The 'bottom line' - what's left after ALL expenses. FORMULA: Net Income = Pre-Tax Income - Taxes

KEY PROFITABILITY RATIOS:

  • Gross Profit Margin: Shows what percentage of revenue remains after paying direct production costs. FORMULA: Gross Profit Margin = Gross Profit ÷ Revenue. EXAMPLE: If revenue is $1,000,000 and COGS is $600,000, then Gross Profit = $400,000 and Gross Profit Margin = 40%. This means for every $1 of sales, the company keeps $0.40 after direct production costs.
  • Operating Profit Margin: Shows what percentage of revenue remains after ALL operating expenses. FORMULA: Operating Profit Margin = Operating Income ÷ Revenue. EXAMPLE: If revenue is $1,000,000 and operating income is $200,000, Operating Profit Margin = 20%.
  • Net Profit Margin: Shows what percentage of revenue becomes actual profit (bottom line). FORMULA: Net Profit Margin = Net Income ÷ Revenue. EXAMPLE: If revenue is $1,000,000 and net income is $100,000, Net Profit Margin = 10%.

REAL EXAMPLE: Let's analyze Apple's income statement (simplified):

  • Revenue: $394 billion
  • COGS: $223 billion
  • Gross Profit: $171 billion (43% margin)
  • Operating Expenses: $51 billion
  • Operating Income: $120 billion (30% margin)
  • Interest & Taxes: $20 billion
  • Net Income: $100 billion (25% margin)
This tells us Apple keeps 43 cents of every dollar after direct costs, 30 cents after all operating costs, and 25 cents as final profit.

EXAM MEMORY TIP: Remember the income statement flows like water falling down stairs: Revenue → Gross Profit → Operating Income → Net Income. Each step subtracts more expenses.
keyPoints: Income statement shows performance OVER A PERIOD OF TIME (not a point in time)
Revenue is the 'top line,' Net Income is the 'bottom line'
Gross Profit = Revenue - Cost of Goods Sold (COGS)
Operating Income = Gross Profit - Operating Expenses
Net Income = Operating Income - Interest - Taxes
Gross Profit Margin = Gross Profit ÷ Revenue
Operating Profit Margin = Operating Income ÷ Revenue
Net Profit Margin = Net Income ÷ Revenue
Higher margins are generally better (but compare within same industry)

variables.COGS: Cost of goods sold (direct production costs)

formulas.expression: Gross Profit Margin = Gross Profit / Revenue
Net Profit Margin = Net Income / Revenue

variables.Net Income: Bottom line profit after all expenses

practiceQuestions.question: A company has revenue of $500,000, COGS of $300,000, and operating expenses of $100,000. What is the company's gross profit?
practiceQuestions.explanation: Gross Profit = Revenue - COGS = $500,000 - $300,000 = $200,000. Operating expenses are NOT subtracted when calculating gross profit - they're subtracted later to get operating income. EXAM TIP: Don't confuse gross profit with operating income. Gross profit only subtracts COGS; operating income also subtracts operating expenses.

practiceQuestions.question: If a company has a gross profit margin of 40% and revenue of $1,000,000, what is its COGS?
practiceQuestions.explanation: Gross Profit Margin = 40% means Gross Profit = 40% × $1,000,000 = $400,000. Since Gross Profit = Revenue - COGS, we can solve: $400,000 = $1,000,000 - COGS. Therefore COGS = $1,000,000 - $400,000 = $600,000. EXAM TIP: You need to know how to work with margins in both directions - from revenue to profit, and from profit margin back to COGS.

sections.title: Balance Sheet Analysis
sections.content:
WHAT THIS MEANS: The balance sheet is a 'snapshot' of the company's financial position at a specific point in time. It shows what the company owns, what it owes, and what's left for shareholders.

THE BALANCE SHEET EQUATION (MUST KNOW):

ASSETS = LIABILITIES + SHAREHOLDERS' EQUITY

This equation MUST always balance - that's why it's called a BALANCE sheet!

THREE MAIN SECTIONS:

  1. ASSETS (What the company OWNS):
    • Current Assets: Expected to be converted to cash or used up within ONE YEAR. EXAMPLES: Cash, accounts receivable (money customers owe), inventory, prepaid expenses.
    • Non-Current Assets (Long-term): Expected to provide benefit for MORE THAN ONE YEAR. EXAMPLES: Property, plant & equipment (PP&E), intangible assets (patents, trademarks), long-term investments.
  2. LIABILITIES (What the company OWES):
    • Current Liabilities: Due within ONE YEAR. EXAMPLES: Accounts payable (money owed to suppliers), short-term debt, accrued expenses, taxes payable.
    • Non-Current Liabilities (Long-term): Due after MORE THAN ONE YEAR. EXAMPLES: Long-term debt, pension obligations, deferred tax liabilities.
  3. SHAREHOLDERS' EQUITY (What's LEFT for owners):
    • Common Stock: Money shareholders invested by buying stock.
    • Retained Earnings: Profits the company kept (didn't pay out as dividends).
    • Additional Paid-In Capital: Extra money shareholders paid above par value.

KEY BALANCE SHEET RATIOS:

  • Current Ratio: Measures ability to pay short-term bills. FORMULA: Current Ratio = Current Assets ÷ Current Liabilities. EXAMPLE: If current assets = $500,000 and current liabilities = $250,000, Current Ratio = 2.0. This means the company has $2 in current assets for every $1 in current liabilities. RULE OF THUMB: Current ratio above 1.5 is generally healthy; below 1.0 is concerning.
  • Quick Ratio (Acid Test): More conservative measure of liquidity (excludes inventory). FORMULA: Quick Ratio = (Current Assets - Inventory) ÷ Current Liabilities. EXAMPLE: If current assets = $500,000, inventory = $200,000, and current liabilities = $250,000, Quick Ratio = ($500,000 - $200,000) ÷ $250,000 = 1.2.
  • Debt-to-Equity Ratio: Measures financial leverage (how much debt vs. equity). FORMULA: Debt-to-Equity = Total Debt ÷ Total Equity. EXAMPLE: If total debt = $1,000,000 and total equity = $2,000,000, D/E = 0.5 or 50%. This means the company uses 50 cents of debt for every $1 of equity.

REAL EXAMPLE: Let's analyze a simple balance sheet:
ASSETSLIABILITIES & EQUITY
Cash: $100,000Accounts Payable: $80,000
Accounts Receivable: $150,000Short-term Debt: $70,000
Inventory: $200,000Total Current Liabilities: $150,000
Total Current Assets: $450,000Long-term Debt: $300,000
PP&E: $550,000Total Liabilities: $450,000
Total Assets: $1,000,000Equity: $550,000
Total Liab. + Equity: $1,000,000 ✓
Current Ratio = $450,000 ÷ $150,000 = 3.0 (very liquid!) Debt-to-Equity = $450,000 ÷ $550,000 = 0.82 (reasonable leverage)

EXAM MEMORY TIP: Remember 'CLANCY' for Current Assets: Cash, Liquid assets, Accounts receivable, Not inventory (yet), Yes inventory. Current assets are listed in order of liquidity (how quickly they can be converted to cash).
keyPoints: Balance sheet is a SNAPSHOT at a point in time (not over a period)
Assets = Liabilities + Equity (MUST ALWAYS BALANCE)
Current assets expected to be used/converted within ONE YEAR
Current liabilities due within ONE YEAR
Current Ratio = Current Assets ÷ Current Liabilities
Quick Ratio = (Current Assets - Inventory) ÷ Current Liabilities
Debt-to-Equity = Total Debt ÷ Total Equity
Current ratio above 1.5 is generally healthy; below 1.0 is concerning
Assets listed in order of liquidity (most liquid first)

formulas.expression: Current Ratio = Current Assets / Current Liabilities

variables.Current Assets: Cash + receivables + inventory + other short-term assets
variables.Current Liabilities: Payables + short-term debt + other obligations due within 1 year

formulas.expression: Quick Ratio = (Current Assets - Inventory) / Current Liabilities

practiceQuestions.question: A company has current assets of $600,000 and current liabilities of $300,000. What is its current ratio?
practiceQuestions.explanation: Current Ratio = Current Assets ÷ Current Liabilities = $600,000 ÷ $300,000 = 2.0. This means the company has $2 in current assets for every $1 in current liabilities, which is a healthy liquidity position. EXAM TIP: Current ratio above 1.0 means the company can pay its short-term bills; above 1.5 is generally considered healthy.

practiceQuestions.question: Which of the following is classified as a CURRENT ASSET?
options: Accounts receivable expected to be collected in 6 months
practiceQuestions.explanation: Accounts receivable expected to be collected in 6 months is a current asset because it will be converted to cash within one year. Property, plant, and equipment (option A) and long-term investments (option C) are non-current assets because they provide benefit for more than one year. EXAM TIP: The key question is: Will this be converted to cash or used up within ONE YEAR? If yes, it's current; if no, it's non-current.

sections.title: Cash Flow Statement Analysis
sections.content:
WHAT THIS MEANS: The cash flow statement shows how cash moved in and out of the company during a period. It answers: 'Where did cash come from, and where did it go?'

WHY CASH FLOW MATTERS: A company can show a PROFIT on the income statement but still run out of CASH. EXAMPLE: A company sells $1 million of products (records revenue) but customers haven't paid yet (all on credit). The income statement shows $1 million profit, but the company has ZERO cash to pay employees and suppliers. Cash flow statement reveals this problem.

THREE SECTIONS OF CASH FLOW STATEMENT:

  1. CASH FLOW FROM OPERATING ACTIVITIES (CFO): Cash from the company's CORE BUSINESS OPERATIONS.
    • Cash INFLOWS (+): Cash from customers, interest received, dividends received
    • Cash OUTFLOWS (-): Cash paid to suppliers, cash paid to employees, interest paid, taxes paid
    • EXAMPLE: If a retail store collects $500,000 from customers and pays $300,000 to suppliers and $100,000 to employees, CFO = +$100,000
    • IMPORTANT: Positive CFO is CRITICAL for long-term survival. Companies can survive with negative investing or financing cash flow, but NOT with negative operating cash flow (for long).
  2. CASH FLOW FROM INVESTING ACTIVITIES (CFI): Cash from buying/selling LONG-TERM ASSETS.
    • Cash INFLOWS (+): Selling property/equipment, selling investments, collecting loan principal
    • Cash OUTFLOWS (-): Buying property/equipment (CapEx), buying investments, lending money
    • EXAMPLE: If a company sells old equipment for $50,000 but buys new equipment for $200,000, CFI = -$150,000
    • IMPORTANT: Negative CFI is often GOOD - it means the company is investing in growth (buying new equipment, expanding).
  3. CASH FLOW FROM FINANCING ACTIVITIES (CFF): Cash from transactions with OWNERS and CREDITORS.
    • Cash INFLOWS (+): Issuing stock, issuing bonds, borrowing from banks
    • Cash OUTFLOWS (-): Paying dividends, repurchasing stock, repaying debt principal
    • EXAMPLE: If a company issues $1 million in bonds, pays $200,000 in dividends, and repays $300,000 of debt, CFF = +$500,000
    • IMPORTANT: Positive CFF means raising capital; negative CFF means returning capital to investors/creditors.

CALCULATING NET CHANGE IN CASH:

Net Change in Cash = CFO + CFI + CFF

Then: Ending Cash = Beginning Cash + Net Change in Cash

REAL EXAMPLE: Let's analyze Apple's cash flow (simplified, in billions):
Cash Flow CategoryAmountWhat It Means
Operating (CFO)+$122 billionCore business generates lots of cash ✓
Investing (CFI)-$22 billionInvesting in equipment, acquisitions
Financing (CFF)-$110 billionPaying dividends, buybacks, repaying debt
Net Change-$10 billionCash decreased slightly
This tells us Apple's core business is a cash machine (+$122B from operations), they're investing in growth (-$22B), and returning massive cash to shareholders (-$110B in dividends and buybacks).

INDIRECT METHOD vs. DIRECT METHOD:

  • Indirect Method (MOST COMMON): Starts with net income and adjusts for non-cash items. EXAMPLE: Net Income: $100M, Add back depreciation: +$20M, Subtract increase in accounts receivable: -$10M → CFO = $110M
  • Direct Method: Lists actual cash receipts and payments. EXAMPLE: Cash from customers: $500M, Cash to suppliers: -$300M, Cash to employees: -$100M
